locked_facts: I_s / I_p = N_p / N_s (current ratio — inverse of voltage ratio)
For ideal transformer: P_input = P_output, i.e., V_p × I_p = V_s × I_s
Step-up transformer: N_s > N_p, V_s > V_p, I_s < I_p
Step-down transformer: N_s < N_p, V_s < V_p, I_s > I_p
Transformer works ONLY on AC — not DC
Core losses (eddy current, hysteresis) cause real transformer efficiency < 100%
Laminated cores reduce eddy current losses
Energy is transferred via changing magnetic flux in the core — mutual inductance
truth_statements: Transformer does not create energy — power in equals power out for ideal transformer
Stepping up voltage steps down current proportionally
Transformer operates only on AC — DC produces no changing flux and no secondary EMF
High voltage transmission reduces current and therefore reduces I²R line losses

aha_moment.what_to_show: Show why power lines use high voltage (400kV) for long-distance transmission. Same power at 400kV means very small current. P = I²R losses in the transmission line depend on I², not V. By stepping voltage up 1000x, current drops 1000x, and line losses drop by 1,000,000x. This is why transformers enable the modern electrical grid.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Two transmission scenarios side by side: 220V at high current (thick wire, glowing red from heat losses) vs 400kV at tiny current (thin wire, cool, minimal loss). Same power delivered, radically different efficiency.

simulation_states.label: Transformer structure — primary and secondary
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Two coils wound on the same iron core. AC source on primary (left). Load on secondary (right). Magnetic flux shown circulating through the core.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: The two coils share the same magnetic core but are NOT electrically connected. Energy is transferred entirely through the changing magnetic flux — mutual inductance in action.

simulation_states.label: Turns ratio determines voltage ratio
simulation_states.what_student_sees: N_p and N_s sliders. V_s = V_p × (N_s/N_p) updates in real time. For default N_p=100, N_s=1000: V_p = 220V → V_s = 2200V (step-up).
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: More turns on secondary = higher voltage. The magnetic flux change per turn is the same in both coils — more turns means more total EMF.

simulation_states.label: Conservation of power — the aha moment
simulation_states.what_student_sees: As V_s increases 10×, I_s decreases 10×. Power meter on both sides shows identical values. When voltage is stepped up, current is stepped down proportionally. Power is conserved.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: A transformer cannot create power. Stepping up voltage is trading current for voltage — the product V×I stays constant. This is the principle behind long-distance power transmission.

simulation_states.label: Why transformers only work on AC
simulation_states.what_student_sees: Switch between AC and DC source. With AC: secondary voltage shown oscillating — transformer works. Switch to DC: secondary voltage = 0 immediately.
simulation_states.what_to_emphasize: A transformer needs changing magnetic flux to induce EMF in the secondary. DC creates a constant field — no change, no induction, no output. This is why AC was chosen for the global electrical grid over Edison's DC.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A step-up transformer increases both voltage and power — it creates extra energy
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Power is conserved in an ideal transformer: V_p × I_p = V_s × I_s. Doubling voltage halves current. Total power unchanged. Energy is never created.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show power meters on both primary and secondary showing identical readings as turns ratio slider is moved. Voltage changes, current changes inversely, power stays constant.

common_misconceptions.student_belief: A transformer can also step up DC voltage (like a battery) to higher values
common_misconceptions.actual_physics: Transformers work only with AC. DC creates a constant magnetic field — no changing flux means no induced EMF in the secondary. A DC-DC converter (which uses electronic switching to create AC internally) is needed for DC voltage conversion.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Connect DC source to primary. Show secondary voltage = 0. Then switch to AC of same amplitude — secondary immediately shows transformed voltage. The on/off of AC is what drives the secondary.

legend.meaning: Turns ratio — determines voltage and current ratios
Current ratio = N_p/N_s (inverse of voltage ratio)
